%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# The Transfer Function from the Frequency Response

> **Section Objective:** Learn how sinusoidal input-output measurements can be used to infer the transfer function of a minimum phase system.

## Concepts In This Section

- Frequency response measurement
- Bode magnitude and phase
- Asymptote slopes
- Corner frequencies
- Minimum phase limitation

## 1. Measuring the Frequency Response

Imagine a system whose internals are completely hidden. You have access only to its input and output terminals. To measure the frequency response, apply a sinusoidal input at a chosen angular frequency \(\omega\), wait for steady state, then record two numbers from the output sinusoid: how much the amplitude changed and how much the phase shifted. Repeat this experiment across many values of \(\omega\), sweeping from low to high.

No knowledge of the internal circuit or differential equation is required — only the terminal behavior matters.

### MINIMAL EXAMPLE

If the input is a sinusoid at \(\omega = 10\) rad/s, and the output has twice the amplitude and lags by \(30^\circ\), that single test gives one frequency-response data point: gain \(= 2\), phase \(= -30^\circ\).

## 3. Corner Frequencies and Minimum Phase

This pair is the measured frequency-response data at one angular frequency \(\omega\).

- \(\left|H(j\omega)\right|\) is the **amplitude gain**: the ratio of output amplitude to input amplitude at frequency \(\omega\).
- \(\angle H(j\omega)\) is the **phase shift**: how much the output sinusoid leads or lags the input.

**When to use it:** Whenever a system is tested with sinusoidal inputs and you record amplitude and phase at each frequency.

**Exam trigger:** Watch for phrases like 'black box', 'sinusoidal input', 'output amplitude', 'phase shift', or 'Bode plot' — these all point to this measured pair.

**Common misuse:** One measured frequency gives only one data point. You cannot determine the complete transfer function from a single \(\omega\) alone.

$$\left(\left|H(j\omega)\right|,\ \angle H(j\omega)\right)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

$$\text{slope} = k\left(20\ \text{dB/decade}\right), \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$
*For any rational transfer function, Bode magnitude asymptote slopes are always **integer multiples of \(20\ \text{dB/decade}\)**.

- \(k\) is an integer counting the net number of active first-order slope contributions at that frequency range.
- Each real pole contributes \(-20\ \text{dB/decade}\) beyond its corner; each real zero contributes \(+20\ \text{dB/decade}\).

**When to use it:** After drawing or reading asymptotes on a Bode magnitude plot, check that every slope segment is a multiple of 20.

**Exam trigger:** Slope changes at corners — for example, a transition from \(0\) to \(-20\ \text{dB/decade}\) signals one pole, and \(-20\) to \(0\ \text{dB/decade}\) signals one zero.

**Common misuse:** Reading slopes directly from a noisy measured curve instead of first fitting the asymptotic straight-line behavior.*

## 3. Corner Frequencies and Minimum Phase

Once the Bode magnitude asymptotes are identified, their breakpoints — the frequencies where the slope changes — are the **corner frequencies**. Each corner frequency is a candidate pole or zero location for the transfer function.

However, this reconstruction is reliable **only for minimum phase systems**. The practical reason: a zero at \(s = a\) (right-half-plane) and a zero at \(s = -a\) (left-half-plane) produce exactly the same magnitude factor when evaluated on the \(j\omega\) axis. Magnitude data alone cannot tell them apart.

### NEAR-MISS TRAP

If two systems share the same Bode magnitude plot but place a zero on opposite sides of the \(s\)-plane, assuming the wrong side gives the wrong transfer function — even though the magnitude evidence looks identical.

$$\left|j\omega - a\right| = \left|j\omega + a\right|$$
*This identity captures the **zero-location ambiguity** at the heart of the minimum phase limitation.

- \(a\) is a positive real number representing the distance of a zero from the origin along the real axis.
- \(j\omega\) is the evaluation point on the imaginary (frequency) axis.
- The left side is the magnitude factor from a right-half-plane zero at \(s = +a\); the right side is from a left-half-plane zero at \(s = -a\). They are equal for every \(\omega\).

**When to use it:** When judging whether a Bode magnitude plot alone can uniquely determine the transfer function.

**Exam trigger:** Any question asking why the Bode reconstruction method works only for minimum phase systems — cite this identity.

**Common misuse:** Assuming that identical magnitude plots always mean identical transfer functions. They do not; the phase plot and the minimum phase assumption are both needed.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Each sinusoidal test yields one data point \(\left(\left|H(j\omega)\right|,\ \angle H(j\omega)\right)\); sweeping \(\omega\) builds the full Bode plot.
- Bode magnitude asymptote slopes follow \(\text{slope} = k(20\ \text{dB/decade})\) for integer \(k\); each slope change marks a corner frequency.
- Corner frequencies identify candidate pole and zero locations; phase data helps resolve the structure.
- The method requires minimum phase: zeros at \(s = \pm a\) satisfy \(\left|j\omega - a\right| = \left|j\omega + a\right|\), so magnitude alone cannot distinguish them.

*Next, we will see how poles shape the frequency response more directly.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
